name: Continuity and Differentiability
subject: Maths

sections.heading: 5.1 Continuity
sections.body:
$f$ is continuous at $x=c$ if $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=f(c)$, i.e. all three of $f(c)$, $\lim_{x\to c^{-}}f(x)$, $\lim_{x\to c^{+}}f(x)$ exist and are equal.

$f$ is continuous on an interval if it is continuous at every point of the interval.

Algebra of continuous functions. If $f,g$ are continuous at $c$, so are $f\pm g$, $fg$, $kf$, and $f/g$ (the latter where $g(c)\neq 0$). Composition of continuous functions is continuous.

All polynomials are continuous on $\mathbb R$. $\sin x,\cos x,e^{x}$ are continuous everywhere; $\tan x$ is continuous wherever defined.

sections.heading: 5.2 Differentiability
sections.body:
$f$ is differentiable at $c$ if $f'(c)=\displaystyle\lim_{h\to 0}\dfrac{f(c+h)-f(c)}{h}$ exists. Differentiability requires the left-hand derivative $\lim_{h\to 0^-}$ and right-hand derivative $\lim_{h\to 0^+}$ to exist and be equal.

Theorem. Differentiable $\Rightarrow$ continuous. Converse is FALSE: $f(x)=|x|$ is continuous at $0$ but not differentiable there.

sections.heading: 5.3 Standard Derivatives & Algebra
sections.body:
$\dfrac{d}{dx}x^{n}=nx^{n-1}$, $\dfrac{d}{dx}\sin x=\cos x$, $\dfrac{d}{dx}\cos x=-\sin x$, $\dfrac{d}{dx}\tan x=\sec^{2}x$, $\dfrac{d}{dx}e^{x}=e^{x}$, $\dfrac{d}{dx}\ln x=\dfrac{1}{x}$.

For inverse trig: $\dfrac{d}{dx}\sin^{-1}x=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}$, $\dfrac{d}{dx}\cos^{-1}x=-\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}$, $\dfrac{d}{dx}\tan^{-1}x=\dfrac{1}{1+x^{2}}$.

Sum/product/quotient. $(f\pm g)'=f'\pm g'$; $(fg)'=f'g+fg'$; $\left(\dfrac{f}{g}\right)'=\dfrac{f'g-fg'}{g^{2}}$.

sections.heading: 5.4 Chain Rule
sections.body:
If $y=f(u)$ and $u=g(x)$ then $\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{dy}{du}\cdot\dfrac{du}{dx}=f'(g(x))\,g'(x).$

Extends to multiple layers: if $y=f(g(h(x)))$, then $y'=f'(g(h(x)))\cdot g'(h(x))\cdot h'(x)$.

sections.heading: 5.5 Implicit, Logarithmic & Parametric Differentiation
sections.body:
Implicit: When $y$ is given implicitly by $F(x,y)=0$, differentiate term-by-term treating $y$ as function of $x$ and apply chain rule, then solve for $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$.

Logarithmic: When $y=[u(x)]^{v(x)}$, take $\ln$: $\ln y=v\ln u$. Differentiate: $\dfrac{1}{y}\dfrac{dy}{dx}=v'\ln u+v\dfrac{u'}{u}$. So $\dfrac{dy}{dx}=y\!\left[v'\ln u+\dfrac{vu'}{u}\right].$

Parametric: If $x=f(t)$, $y=g(t)$, then $\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{dy/dt}{dx/dt}=\dfrac{g'(t)}{f'(t)}$, $f'(t)\neq 0$.

sections.heading: 5.6 Second-Order Derivatives
sections.body:
$y''=\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}=\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right).$

For parametric $x=f(t)$, $y=g(t)$: differentiate $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ w.r.t. $t$ and divide by $\dfrac{dx}{dt}$. So $\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}=\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)\Big/\dfrac{dx}{dt}.$

sections.heading: 5.7 Mean Value Theorems
sections.body:
Rolle's Theorem. If $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$, differentiable on $(a,b)$, and $f(a)=f(b)$, then there exists $c\in(a,b)$ with $f'(c)=0$.

Lagrange's Mean Value Theorem. If $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$ and differentiable on $(a,b)$, then there exists $c\in(a,b)$ with $f'(c)=\dfrac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}.$

Geometrically: there is a tangent at some interior point parallel to the chord joining the endpoints.

summary: Continuity at $c$ requires $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=f(c)$. Differentiability requires the symmetric difference quotient to have a finite limit, and implies continuity (not vice versa). Master sum/product/quotient, chain rule, implicit, logarithmic and parametric differentiation, and the standard derivatives table including inverse trig. Second derivatives stack the rule. Rolle's and Lagrange's MVT relate average and instantaneous rates of change.
pitfalls: Concluding differentiability from continuity — the converse is false ($|x|$ at 0).
Forgetting the inner derivative in the chain rule.
Misapplying the quotient rule sign — numerator is $f'g-fg'$, NOT $fg'-f'g$.
Using $\frac{d}{dx}u^{v}=v u^{v-1}u'$ when $v$ depends on $x$ — must use logarithmic differentiation.
Skipping the parametric chain rule and writing $\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}=\frac{d^{2}y/dt^{2}}{d^{2}x/dt^{2}}$ (wrong).
